response:
The error you are encountering occurs because your dynamically loaded assembly is trying to reference WPF assemblies that are not available in the .NET 7 runtime on Windows in the way your project is currently configured. WPF is a Windows-specific technology that relies on the Windows Presentation Foundation, which is not part of the core .NET 7 cross-platform libraries.

When you compile your WPF project for Windows, it likely targets a specific framework version or includes references that expect the full .NET Framework environment rather than the modern .NET 6 or 7 SDK style. This mismatch causes the runtime to look for PresentationFramework in a location or format that does not exist in your cross-platform .NET 7 application.

cousin to this issue is the fact that WPF simply cannot run on macOS, so your strategy of conditionally compiling or loading the WPF project only on Windows is logically sound but technically flawed in implementation. You need to ensure that the WPF-specific code is isolated in a separate class library that is only referenced when the target platform is Windows.

To resolve this without changing your main application to .NET Framework, you should create a separate class library project that targets .NET 7 and includes the WPF references, then use conditional compilation or runtime checks to load this library only on Windows. Ensure your main application uses reflection or a dependency injection container to load this library dynamically, preventing the cross-platform build from including WPF dependencies.

Finally, verify that your project file for the WPF library explicitly sets the TargetFramework to net7.0-windows and includes the necessary WPF SDK references, while your main application remains a standard net7.0 project. This separation allows the macOS build to ignore the WPF library entirely, while the Windows build correctly loads the required PresentationFramework assemblies at runtime.
